title: The burden of ESKAPE pathogen-related hospital-acquired infections: clinical and financial perspective from a systematic review.
body:
Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) poses a significant challenge in managing hospital-acquired infections (HAIs) caused by ESKAPE pathogens. We conducted a systematic literature search in Embase, Web of Science, and PubMed for articles published between 2000 and 2023, to assess the clinical and economic burden of ESKAPE infections. The evidence covered mortality rates, length of hospital stays (LOS), and healthcare costs, with most studies focusing on HAIs caused by methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). These impacts varied by region, ESKAPE pathogen strain, and type of resistance. Compared to non-resistant groups, resistant ESKAPE-related HAIs were associated with nearly double the risk of death (odds ratio [OR] = 1.96; 95% confidence interval [CI] = 1.56-2.46). Resistant ESKAPE-related HAIs also result in significantly longer hospital LOS (p <0.001) and increased healthcare costs (p = 0.047), particularly in the Asia-Pacific region. The study highlights the urgent need for comprehensive strategies and global collaboration to mitigate the impact of AMR, particularly in resource-constrained settings. Addressing these challenges can improve patient outcomes and reduce the financial burden on healthcare systems worldwide.

TL;DR: The study highlights the urgent need for comprehensive strategies and global collaboration to mitigate the impact of AMR, particularly in resource-constrained settings, and can improve patient outcomes and reduce the financial burden on healthcare systems worldwide.
